title: Cuban sandwich
subtitle: Pressed sandwich of roast pork, ham, Swiss cheese, and pickles
excerpt: The Cuban sandwich: sliced roast pork and ham layered with Swiss cheese, pickles, and mustard in a ciabatta roll, then pressed flat in a hot pan until the bread is crisp and the cheese melts. Specific and excellent.
sourceNotes: The Cuban sandwich (Cubano) originated in the Cuban communities of Tampa and Miami in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The traditional version uses Cuban bread (pan cubano), which has a slightly sweet, soft crumb and very thin crust. Ciabatta is the closest widely available substitute in the UK. The filling is roast pork, ham (jamón), Swiss cheese (Emmental or Gruyère), dill pickles, and yellow mustard. The sandwich is pressed flat on a plancha or sandwich press until the bread is golden and the cheese melts completely. This version uses gammon steak as the ham component; any cooked ham slice works.

content.text: The Cuban sandwich is one of the great pressed sandwiches: specific in its components, specific in its method, and very good when made correctly. The roast pork can be leftovers from lechón asado or any slow-roasted pork. The pressing is not optional — it is what melts the cheese, compresses the layers, and crisps the bread into something more structured than a soft roll.

The correct order of assembly is important: mustard on both halves of the bread, then Swiss cheese on the bottom half, then ham, then roast pork, then pickles, then the top half. The cheese on the bottom melts against the bread; the pickles go above the pork so their moisture doesn't make the bread soggy before pressing.

items.prepNote: halved lengthways — Cuban bread if available
cooked and thinly sliced — lechón asado leftovers are ideal
cooked or grilled, or any cooked ham
or yellow mustard for the traditional version

content.text: Spread the {{dijon-mustard}} of mustard on the cut face of each half of the ciabatta. Layer the {{emmental}} of emmental on the bottom half, followed by the {{gammon-steak}} gammon, then the {{pork-loin}} of sliced pork, then the {{gherkins}} gherkin slices. Close the sandwich. Butter the outside of both cut sides with the {{unsalted-butter}} of soft butter.

Heat a cast-iron skillet over medium heat until hot. Place the sandwich in the pan. Lay a second heavy pan or a foil-wrapped brick on top to press the sandwich flat. Cook for 3–4 minutes until the bottom is golden. Flip carefully, replace the weight, and cook the other side for 3 minutes until the bread is golden all over and the cheese is completely melted. Cut diagonally and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Cheese not melting before bread burns
items.cause: Heat too high; cheese sliced too thick
items.fix: Use medium heat and slice the cheese thinly. The cheese takes longer to melt than the bread takes to brown. A lid placed over the pan for 1 minute during pressing traps heat and speeds melting.

items.cause: Pan not hot enough; no butter on outside
items.fix: The butter on the outside of the bread is what crisps it. Press firmly — the weight compresses the sandwich and increases contact with the hot surface.

items.cause: Too much filling; not pressed firmly enough
items.fix: The pressing is what consolidates the layers. Apply firm, even pressure. Cut cleanly through the compressed sandwich with a sharp knife.

items.cause: Roast pork not moist enough; sliced too thin
items.fix: Thinly sliced pork in a pressed sandwich needs some fat to stay moist. The fattier layers from lechón asado or a slow-cooked shoulder are better than lean loin here.

content.text: The Cuban sandwich is a diaspora creation: it emerged in the Cuban communities of Tampa and Miami in the late nineteenth century, made by Cuban workers for Cuban workers at lunchtime. Tampa claims the original, with the addition of salami; Miami's version omits it. Both are pressed, and both use roast pork and ham together. The sandwich travelled back to Cuba, where it became popular in Havana. Outside the US and Cuba it is eaten mainly in cities with significant Cuban populations. Made well, it is one of the best sandwiches that exists.
